text:
Atlassian is teaming up with Lovable to help makers, product designers, and developers across all teams spend less time on busywork and more time building what’s next.

With this partnership, now you can:

✨ Instantly transform Jira tickets or Confluence docs into working prototypes using Lovable’s API—bridging the gap between planning and building.

✨ Enrich Lovable prompts with real-time Atlassian content, so AI-powered workflows always have the right context and insights.

Atlassian’s ongoing investment in AI and open integrations means every builder can ideate and iterate faster, accelerating how they deliver value to customers.

What will you build?

Every site published on Lovable is now scanned by Guardio for phishing, scams, and impersonation.

As AI-powered tools democratize digital creation, we ensure these new tools are not used to cause harm.

comments.text: Imagine you could verify who or which platform is a scam? This wasn’t possible in thr past! We are building a decentralised solution with verifiable credentials. Based on real interactions between services, users, platforms and devices. We could talk Lovable! We’d love to support in this journey to make a safer Internet together!
This is fantastic!

This is the kind of proactive integrity the AI ecosystem needs more of. At Insyghtful.ai, where we build emotionally intelligent tools for sales teams, we know that trust isn’t just earned through features it’s built through safeguards. Lovable’s integration with Guardio is a quiet but powerful signal: creativity at scale must come with accountability. Respect for leading with responsibility.

text:
Lovable is on a mission to democratize building anything digital - but not for scammers and threat actors! 
Today, we partner with Guardio to analyze the content and behaviour of all published Lovable pages, so that we can take down any malicious content that managed to evade our guardrails in near real-time. Guardio combines the static analysis of public website content and intelligence from more than 2M threat detection sensors online.

The Golden Age of the Design Founder 🔥

There’s a pattern I keep noticing in the most successful founders, and it’s not what I expected. It’s not that they’re engineers or designers, it’s that the best ones think like designers, regardless of their background.

This matters because it contradicts the conventional wisdom about what makes a good founder. For decades, the archetypal founder was an engineer. There were good reasons for this. Starting a company meant building something, and engineers were the people who could build things. But I think we’ve been confusing a historical accident with a law of nature.

The thing about historical accidents is that they persist long after the conditions that created them disappear. And the conditions that made engineering the obvious path to founding are disappearing faster than most people realize.

What’s actually hard now:

Here’s what’s really happening: the bottleneck in startups is shifting from “can you build it” to “do you know what to build.” That might sound like a subtle distinction, but it changes everything.

When the bottleneck was building, engineers had an obvious advantage. They could turn ideas into reality without the coordination cost of explaining what they wanted to someone else. Speed mattered more than precision. You needed to iterate fast, and iteration was fastest when you could code.

But as tools improve (and they’re improving exponentially now), the ability to build is becoming less of a constraint. It’s not that building is easy, it’s that the gap between having a clear vision and manifesting it is shrinking. What’s becoming more valuable is the clarity of vision itself.

This is where something interesting reveals itself. The engineers who succeed aren’t just good at building - they’re good at knowing what to build. They think about user experience. They obsess over details. They have strong opinions about how things should feel. In other words, they think like designers, even if they came up through engineering.

I’m seeing this strongly at Lovable. Both founders are engineers. But what makes them effective isn’t just their technical ability but the way they’re deeply involved in every product decision. They care about the experience, not just the functionality. They can build anything, but more importantly, they know what’s worth building.

Read full article on my blog: https://lnkd.in/eTxW5WeE

As a Project Manager, I see AI as a powerful enabler enhancing capability, accelerating analysis, and improving decision-making. It lets me focus on strategy, leadership, and delivering real value instead of repetitive admin.
Mastering Prompt Engineering is essential. It bridges human intent and machine intelligence, turning AI systems like GPT into real value in project delivery. Used well, it enables rapid scenario analysis, stronger risk evaluation, and clearer stakeholder reporting.
Example of a short but well engineered prompt:
 "Act as a Senior IT Project Manager. Solve a decision-making problem using Decision Tree Analysis for two Engineering projects, each needing £30m investment. Include an overview on its value in Project Management. Project A: 55% chance of high demand with £50m revenue and 45% low demand at £20m. Project B: 40% chance of exceptional demand at £60m and 60% moderate demand at £30m. Compare projects in one table and calculate EMV for each."
The key to effective Prompt Engineering is RACFT: Role, Ask, Context, Format, Tone. But it only works when guided by genuine industry knowledge. Without real expertise, even the best prompt will fail to deliver. AI succeeds most when paired knowledge and experience

comments.text:
Great blog post! To say Atlas changes the game would be an understatement. It shifts the frame from “How do we search the internet?” to “How do we interact with it?” It goes from search engine to action engine. I’m not sure the world fully understands the history we’re witnessing in real time.

By decoupling the engine from the application layer, you’ve created a foundation that supports agentic use, safety boundaries, and contextual decision-making at scale. That’s not just a product improvement, it’s a new layer of infrastructure.

This shift matters not only to users, but to policymakers, educators, and institutions who will need AI systems that are reliable, transparent, and consistent across environments. As we learn to use and adapt to AI at scale, we don’t need to romanticize or villainize its growth. We need to build it honestly, acknowledge the tradeoffs, debate them in public, and anchor the outcomes in trust. When safety leads, progress can be shared.

This is the beginning of a new civic interface, not just a new browser. 

Congratulations to the team. This is a moment.

text:
One of the things I’m most excited about with ChatGPT is how it’s revolutionizing healthcare. GPT-5 is our best model yet for answering health questions. It scores significantly higher than any previous model on HealthBench, an evaluation OpenAI published earlier this year based on realistic scenarios in partnership with over 250 physicians. 
Felipe, a member of our OpenAI team, and his wife Carolina shared their story on today’s livestream, and it really moved me. Last October, Carolina was hit with three cancer diagnoses in one week. Like many, her biopsy results felt overwhelming and confusing. On a whim, she took a screenshot of the biopsy report and put it into ChatGPT. Within seconds, it translated the medical jargon into plain language, helping her feel more in control and more prepared to talk to her doctor about next steps. She’s kept using ChatGPT throughout her cancer journey, even to help make a tough choice about radiation when doctors gave conflicting advice. It’s incredible to hear how ChatGPT restored her sense of agency during these critical decisions. You can watch the full livestream and their story here:

article.title: ChronicleBio | AI-Powered Data Platform for Complex Conditions

text:
Last month I gave an update on the journey I’ve been on to find cures for complex chronic conditions like the ones I suffer from. Today, I’m thrilled to announce that we’re launching ChronicleBio — a company entirely dedicated to building the world’s best data platform on neuroimmune conditions like POTS, ME-CFS and Long COVID, that affect 250+ millions of people.

I’m honored to be co-founding ChronicleBio with Rohit Gupta and Rishi Reddy, who are the ones running the business full-time. The three of us are patients and caregivers in this space, so we have deep personal commitment to the mission.

For most conditions, like cancer or Parkinson's, critical datasets already exist and have massively accelerated the path to drugs. But for neuroimmune conditions, these datasets are sorely missing, making it hard for biotech and pharma to invest in developing drugs.

The advances we’re seeing in AI make me more confident than ever that we’re on the cusp of being able to cure these diseases. But powerful models alone aren’t enough — we also need rich, high quality, deeply-analyzed biological data that these AI models can be trained or deployed on.

ChronicleBio is here to provide exactly that.
We are bringing together providers, pharma and patients to generate the world’s largest and deepest multi-modal data set for our patient population, with the goal of surfacing new biological patterns, building biomarkers, and ultimately accelerating the path to diagnostics and treatments.

Within months we have already begun launching clinical partnerships, building a scalable data ingestion engine and biobanking infrastructure, generating brand awareness with relevant patient advocacy groups, generating revenue with pharma via a decentralized clinical trial network and assembling a world class mission-oriented team of AI technologists, scientists and healthcare leaders.

This work is personal. Behind every sample we collect, there is a patient suffering, fainting in random places, stuck in bed for years, or living with relentless, invisible symptoms. These conditions are profoundly debilitating — and yet there are still no validated biomarkers, no diagnostics, no cures. That is unacceptable.

This is a long road, but like I’ve said before: I will not rest until these conditions are eradicated. In the meantime, the team at ChronicleBio will build the data infrastructure that will make tomorrow’s cures possible, faster.

comments.text:
Hey Tobias, long time no chat.

Respectfully, what's the data use policy, and how will sustainable development thresholds be implemented for use in this capacity? 

What I mean by the latter is: how can you ensure market saturation without oversaturating, a process that leads to over farming of  the landscape.

Much like crops fail to thrive when not rotated to allow nutrient replenishment, untenable cost of living can lead to pricing the customers/supply out of the market for your clients.

There are no sales if there are no buyers and there are no buyers if everyone's income is tied up in base survival, like food or shelter.

This is especially prevalent in Canada, with a growing demographic segment struggling to afford the basic means for survival, let alone buy from entrepreneurs the "nice to have" products. More people have to buy from supercenters to minimize transportation costs, shipping, and delivery, and frankly that helps the environment too, but when the clients can't afford the services because the customers can't afford their products, where does that leave you other than holding all of that data from sales, but no one is able to maintain purchase expectations?

We can't get blood from a stone, as the old adage goes.

Unconventional greatness and mastery is not tied to, or guaranteed by, a conventional degree, title, or qualification. 

Unconventionally, Shopify has always recognised that. The world’s best beyond our core disciplines have always been welcomed to join Shopify, including chess grandmasters, Reddit influencers, top athletes, and pro gamers. 

Unconventionally, the Shopify careers site now has a VIP Access gateway to make that even clearer. Candidates with world-class skills and globally-unrivaled expertise can now be fast-tracked without making an application to a defined role. We replaced a traditional application form with the opportunity to ‘get sourced’ instead.

Unconventionally, the copy line ‘Can we slide into your DMs?’ was written by the dev on the team; a team of 3 people yolo-shipped the VIP Access page concept and content during a 2-day team event.

comments.text:
From a cybersecurity & security testing perspective, this looks very promising.
 Being able to let an agent control the browser and capture screenshots means:
* Faster reproduction of user-side issues during penetration tests or bug hunting
* Automated checks of web app flows (login, session handling, redirects)
* Easier collection of visual evidence for audit and compliance reports

For security teams, this could cut down the manual effort of simulating attacks or documenting findings.
 Of course, the key will be how well permissions and isolation are handled — but if done right, this is a serious productivity boost for anyone testing web applications.
